Without the 'always' keyword: the router only advertises the default IF it has a default route in its own routing table. If the static default is removed or the ISP link fails, OSPF stops advertising — desirable behavior (don't promise a path that doesn't work).

Verification: on other routers, show ip route 0.0.0.0 should show the O*E2 route. show ip route output should include 'Gateway of last resort is X.X.X.X to network 0.0.0.0' at the top.

nodes: All OSPF routers learn O*E2 default

hack.memory: 'default-information originate' under OSPF = 'advertise my default route to everyone.' Requires existing default in routing table (without 'always'). Shows up as O*E2 0.0.0.0/0 on other routers. Sets gateway of last resort.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: on the edge router, configure 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [ISP-IP]' then 'router ospf 1' -> 'default-information originate.' On internal routers, run 'show ip route' — should show 'O*E2 0.0.0.0/0' and 'Gateway of last resort is set.' Ping the ISP IP from an internal router to verify reachability.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 21. Exam: GUARANTEED — 'how do you advertise a default route into OSPF?' -> default-information originate.

3.4.h.info:
default-information originate (without the always keyword) only advertises the default route when the router already has a default route in its own routing table. If the edge router's static default is removed — or the next-hop becomes unreachable — OSPF stops advertising the default. Internal routers lose the O*E2 route and their gateway of last resort.

Why this is safer: you only tell others about the internet path when you actually have one. If your ISP link dies and the static default is removed, your router is HONEST about not being able to reach the internet. Other routers immediately remove the broken path and can fall back to an alternate edge router (if another 'default-information originate' source exists).

Failure scenario:

  1. Edge router has ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1 and default-information originate.
  2. ISP link fails — the static default is removed because the next-hop 203.0.113.1 becomes unreachable.
  3. OSPF removes the default from its LSDB.
  4. Internal routers lose O*E2 0.0.0.0/0 from their routing tables.
  5. Internal routers fall back to a secondary default (if another OSPF router is advertising one) OR have no default — internet traffic is black-holed until recovery.

Design best practice: if you have multiple edge routers (active/standby or load-balancing), all should use default-information originate (NOT always). OSPF SPF automatically picks the best path; when one fails, the remaining one advertises — seamless failover.

Verification: on edge router, show ip route static confirms the static default exists. Remove it (no ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0) and watch internal routers lose the O*E2.

hack.memory: Without 'always' = OSPF advertises the default ONLY if the edge router HAS one. Lose the static default -> stop advertising -> internal routers lose the path honestly. Safer for multi-edge designs.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: set up 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop]' and 'default-information originate' on the edge router. Internal routers have O*E2. Remove the static default (no ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop]). Run 'show ip route' on internal routers — O*E2 is GONE. Re-add static route -> O*E2 returns.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 21. Exam: know the 'default route in table required' behavior without 'always.'

3.4.h.info:
default-information originate always advertises the default route unconditionally, even if the router does NOT have a default route in its own table. Use when the router will always be the internet exit point (e.g., sole WAN router) and you want internal routers to always keep a path pointing at it, regardless of the edge's own state.

Configuration:

router ospf 1
 default-information originate always

Risk: the router may advertise a default even when its own ISP link is DOWN. Internal routers continue sending traffic to the edge, where it's silently dropped. This is called a black hole — traffic disappears without returning errors.

When to use it: rarely. Specific cases:

  • BGP-to-OSPF redistribution: the edge router learns the default from BGP but the admin wants it guaranteed in OSPF regardless.
  • Single-edge topologies: only one path exists; there's no alternate, so advertising the default even when broken is operationally acceptable (the alternative would be no default at all).
  • Testing/lab: to quickly force a default injection without setting up upstream routing first.

Without 'always' is preferred for production multi-edge designs because it provides accurate failover. 'Always' is a 'trust me, I'm always up' advertisement — only use it when that claim is truthful.

Exam tip: know the distinction. 'Always' = advertise regardless. Without 'always' = advertise only if default route exists locally.

hack.memory: 'always' = advertise regardless of local default. Less safe (might black-hole traffic) but guaranteed advertisement. Use only when the edge is truly always-on or when BGP provides the default elsewhere. Default preference: WITHOUT 'always.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: without 'always', remove the static default -> O*E2 disappears on internal routers. Now use 'default-information originate always' on the edge router with NO static default route configured. 'show ip route' on the edge shows NO default, but internal routers still see O*E2. This is the black-hole risk in action.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 21. Exam trap: given a scenario where default-information originate is configured but no static default exists — does the default propagate? Answer: only if 'always' is present.

3.4.h.info:
O*E2 0.0.0.0/0 is how the OSPF-injected default route appears on receiving routers. Breaking down the codes:

  • O — learned from OSPF.
  • * — candidate default route. When installed, this route sets 'Gateway of last resort.'
  • E2 — External Type 2 LSA. External = originated by redistribution (the edge's static default was injected into OSPF). Type 2 = external cost does NOT increment as it traverses the OSPF domain.
  • 0.0.0.0/0 — the default route prefix. Matches any destination that doesn't have a more specific route.

E1 vs E2 (critical distinction):

  • O E2 (default): cost = the ASBR's redistribute metric only. Internal path costs do NOT add. A distant router sees the same metric as a close one.
  • O E1: cost = ASBR's redistribute metric + internal cost to reach the ASBR. Accumulates hop-by-hop.

E2 is the default for default-information originate. You can change to E1 with default-information originate metric-type 1. E2 is usually fine; E1 is used when you want internal routers to prefer closer exit points.

Full routing table entry example:

O*E2 0.0.0.0/0 [110/1] via 10.0.0.1, 00:05:23, GigabitEthernet0/0
Gateway of last resort is 10.0.0.1 to network 0.0.0.0

AD 110, metric 1 (default E2 metric set by the ASBR). Gateway of last resort line is printed when a candidate default is installed.

hack.memory: O*E2 0.0.0.0/0 = OSPF-learned external type 2 candidate default. O = OSPF, * = candidate default, E2 = external cost doesn't increment. E1 vs E2: E1 accumulates internal cost, E2 doesn't. Default is E2.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: after 'default-information originate' on the edge router, on an internal router run 'show ip route' — find the O*E2 entry and 'Gateway of last resort.' Change to E1 with 'default-information originate metric-type 1' — internal routers now show O*E1 instead. Observe how the metric field changes.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 21. Exam may ask 'what does O*E2 mean?' -> OSPF candidate default, external type 2.

3.4.h.info:
A distinguishing property of OSPF External Type 2 (E2) routes: the cost does NOT change as the LSA is flooded through the OSPF domain. Every router, no matter how close or far from the ASBR (the originating router), sees the same metric — the value set by the ASBR at redistribution time.

Example: edge router R1 originates O*E2 0.0.0.0/0 with metric 1. R2 (one hop from R1) sees metric 1. R5 (four hops from R1) also sees metric 1. The internal OSPF path cost (sum of interface costs to reach R1) is NOT added. This is different from normal intra-area OSPF routes (where cost sums).

E1 vs E2 in multi-edge design: if two edge routers both originate E2 defaults with metric 1, internal routers can't distinguish which is closer — metrics are identical. Internal routers pick based on Router ID tiebreaker or load-balance across both. With E1, each internal router adds its path cost to reach each edge, so closer edges win naturally.

When E2 is fine:

  • Single-edge network — no need to differentiate closer/farther edges.
  • Multi-edge with equal-cost load balancing desired (all paths look equal).
  • Simplicity — you don't want internal path cost to influence exit choice.

When to use E1:

  • Multi-edge designs where you want each internal router to prefer its geographically closest edge.
  • When you need path-cost-aware external routing (like dual-ISP with latency differences).

Configure: default-information originate metric-type [1|2] or redistribute static subnets metric-type [1|2].

hack.memory: E2 = metric FROZEN. Every OSPF router sees the same cost to the ASBR, regardless of hops. E1 = metric ACCUMULATES (internal cost added to external). Default for 'default-information originate' is E2.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer with multi-area or multi-hop OSPF: originate O*E2 default. On routers at different hop counts from the edge, run 'show ip route 0.0.0.0' — all show the same metric. Change to E1 with 'default-information originate metric-type 1' — now metrics differ per router based on path cost.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 21. Exam rarely asks E1 vs E2 directly but expects you to know E2 metric is constant.

3.4.i.info:
show ip ospf neighbor is the single most important OSPF verification command. It displays every OSPF neighbor the router has, their state, role, priority, and dead-timer countdown. Read this command first when troubleshooting OSPF.

Output fields:

  • Neighbor ID: the Router ID of the neighbor (not their interface IP).
  • Pri: OSPF priority of the neighbor's interface (for DR/BDR election).
  • State: adjacency state + DR role. Formats: FULL/DR, FULL/BDR, FULL/DROTHER, 2WAY/DROTHER, FULL/ - (P2P).
  • Dead Time: countdown until this neighbor is declared dead. Resets to 40 (or 120 on NBMA) whenever a Hello arrives.
  • Address: the neighbor's interface IP on our side.
  • Interface: the local interface connecting to this neighbor.

Example output:

R1# show ip ospf neighbor
Neighbor ID   Pri   State      Dead Time   Address    Interface
2.2.2.2        1    FULL/DR    00:00:32    10.0.0.2   GigabitEthernet0/0
3.3.3.3        1    FULL/BDR   00:00:35    10.0.0.3   GigabitEthernet0/0
4.4.4.4        1    2WAY/DROTHER 00:00:38   10.0.0.4   GigabitEthernet0/0

Troubleshooting signals:

  • FULL state: healthy adjacency, LSDBs synced.
  • 2-Way on broadcast: normal for DROther-to-DROther pairs.
  • Stuck Init: one-way Hellos (ACL, passive, unidirectional link).
  • Stuck ExStart: MTU mismatch (classic).
  • Missing neighbor: Hello timer mismatch, area mismatch, or subnet mask mismatch.

hack.memory: 'show ip ospf neighbor' = OSPF's daily health check. Full = healthy. Init = one-way. ExStart = MTU. 2-Way on broadcast = normal for DROthers. Dead Time counting down = neighbor alive. Missing = check HAMSS.
hack.practice: Every OSPF lab: first command is 'show ip ospf neighbor.' Parse every field — RID, priority, state, dead time, interface. Build a troubleshooting table: 'state X means problem Y.' Memorize healthy vs stuck states.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24-26. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam: output-interpretation question where you diagnose from 'show ip ospf neighbor.' Memorize every state.

3.4.i.info:
show ip ospf interface brief provides a compact summary of all OSPF-enabled interfaces on the router. One line per interface. Great for quickly answering 'what interfaces is OSPF running on, what area, what cost?'

Output columns:

  • Interface: name (e.g., Gi0/0).
  • PID: OSPF process ID.
  • Area: OSPF area this interface is in.
  • IP Address/Mask: interface IP.
  • Cost: OSPF cost on this interface.
  • State: DR, BDR, DROTHER, P2P, or WAIT (during election).
  • Nbrs F/C: Full neighbors / Count — e.g., '2/2' means 2 neighbors fully adjacent out of 2 total.

Example output:

R1# show ip ospf interface brief
Interface   PID  Area  IP Address/Mask   Cost  State  Nbrs F/C
Gi0/0       1    0     10.0.0.1/24       10    DR     2/2
Gi0/1       1    0     192.168.1.1/24    100   DR     0/0
Se0/0/0     1    0     10.1.1.1/30       64    P2P    1/1

Quick diagnostic:

  • Unexpected area? Interface in wrong area — adjacency won't form with routers in area 0.
  • Cost 1 on Gi0/0 but should be 10? auto-cost reference-bandwidth is default (100 Mbps) — increase it.
  • Nbrs F/C = 0/0 but should be 1/1? Passive interface, ACL, or configuration issue.
  • State WAIT? Election in progress; wait 40s for Dead interval to expire.

Related: show ip ospf interface [specific-int] — detailed view of one interface, including timers, DR/BDR, network type, passive status.

hack.memory: 'show ip ospf interface brief' = one-liner per OSPF interface. Fast overview: process, area, IP, cost, state, neighbor count. F/C column shows Full/Count — 1/1 is good, 0/0 is bad (unless passive).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure OSPF on 3 interfaces. Run 'show ip ospf interface brief' — verify all show area 0, correct costs, correct states. Make one interface passive; run again — neighbor count drops to 0/0 on that interface.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24-26. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam: show command interpretation.

3.4.i.info:
show ip ospf interface [interface] displays detailed OSPF information for one specific interface. Much more data than the brief version. This is the go-to command for deep OSPF interface debugging.

Key fields in the output:

  • Interface state: 'GigabitEthernet0/0 is up, line protocol is up.'
  • Internet Address: interface IP/mask.
  • Area ID: the OSPF area.
  • Process ID: OSPF process number.
  • Router ID: THIS router's RID (useful to confirm).
  • Network Type: BROADCAST, POINT_TO_POINT, NON_BROADCAST, or POINT_TO_MULTIPOINT.
  • Cost: OSPF cost for this interface.
  • Transmit Delay: default 1 second.
  • State: DR, BDR, DROTHER, or WAITING.
  • Designated Router / Backup Designated Router: RID and interface IP of each.
  • Timer intervals: 'Hello 10, Dead 40, Wait 40, Retransmit 5.'
  • Hello due in: seconds until next Hello.
  • Neighbor Count: total neighbors discovered on this interface.
  • Adjacent neighbor count: neighbors in Full state.
  • Passive interface flag: 'No Hellos (Passive interface)' if passive.
  • MTU: not in this command; use 'show ip interface' for MTU.

Common uses:

  • Verify timers match expected values.
  • Confirm network type (e.g., after ip ospf network point-to-point).
  • Identify the DR/BDR.
  • Check if passive (if adjacency not forming).
  • Verify area assignment.

hack.memory: 'show ip ospf interface [int]' = deep dive per interface. See timers, network type, DR/BDR, cost, passive status, neighbor count. Use for root-cause analysis. 'show ip ospf interface brief' is the fast version; this is the detail version.
hack.practice: On any OSPF-enabled interface: run 'show ip ospf interface Gi0/0' and identify EVERY field. Modify one setting (e.g., 'ip ospf hello-interval 20') and verify the output updates. This builds command-output fluency, which the exam rewards.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24-26. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam: output-interpretation and timer/state verification questions.

3.4.i.info:
show ip ospf displays OSPF process-level information — Router ID, area configuration, SPF statistics, reference bandwidth, and other process-wide settings. Run this to quickly verify global OSPF config.

Key fields:

  • Routing Process 'ospf 1' with ID X.X.X.X: the process ID and the active Router ID.
  • Number of areas in this router is N: how many areas this router has interfaces in (normally 1 for single-area).
  • Reference bandwidth unit is X Mbps: the current reference bandwidth (default 100; recommended 10000).
  • It is an area border router: appears if this router is an ABR (multi-area only).
  • It is an autonomous system boundary router: appears if this router redistributes external routes or has default-information originate.
  • Number of external LSAs: count of Type 5 external LSAs in the LSDB.
  • SPF algorithm executed N times: total SPF calculations since process start.
  • Area 0: Number of interfaces in this area is N, Number of fully adjacent neighbors in this area is M.

Common diagnostic uses:

  • Confirm the Router ID is what you configured.
  • Verify reference bandwidth matches other routers.
  • Check if this router is ASBR (should be, if originating default).
  • Compare number of fully adjacent neighbors vs expected neighbor count.

Example output:

R1# show ip ospf
 Routing Process 'ospf 1' with ID 1.1.1.1
 Reference bandwidth unit is 10000 Mbps
 Number of areas in this router is 1. 1 normal 0 stub 0 nssa
 Area BACKBONE(0)
  Number of interfaces in this area is 3
  Number of fully adjacent neighbors in this area is 2

hack.memory: 'show ip ospf' = process-level summary. RID, reference bandwidth, area count, ABR/ASBR role, adjacency count. Use to verify globals. If reference BW doesn't match other routers -> fix it.
hack.practice: On each OSPF router in your lab: run 'show ip ospf' and verify (1) Router ID is what you set, (2) reference bandwidth matches the chosen domain value, (3) fully adjacent neighbor count = expected count. Any discrepancy = investigate.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam: output-reading questions for RID and reference bandwidth verification.

3.4.i.info:
show ip route ospf filters the routing table to display only OSPF-learned routes. Fast way to see what OSPF has installed without the clutter of connected, static, or other protocols.

Route codes you'll see:

  • O — intra-area route (same area as the source).
  • O IA — inter-area route (learned via an ABR; multi-area only).
  • O E1 — external type 1 (metric accumulates across OSPF domain).
  • O E2 — external type 2 (metric constant; default for redistributed routes).
  • O N1 / O N2 — NSSA external type 1/2 (multi-area NSSA only; not on CCNA).
  • O*E1 / O*E2 — external candidate default route (0.0.0.0/0). The '*' marks it.

Example output:

R2# show ip route ospf
O   10.2.0.0/24 [110/20] via 10.0.0.1, 00:10:15, GigabitEthernet0/0
O   10.3.0.0/24 [110/30] via 10.0.0.1, 00:10:15, GigabitEthernet0/0
O*E2 0.0.0.0/0 [110/1] via 10.0.0.1, 00:05:30, GigabitEthernet0/0

Interpretation:

  • [110/20]: AD 110, metric 20. Metric = sum of OSPF interface costs along the path.
  • via 10.0.0.1: next hop (the neighbor's interface IP).
  • GigabitEthernet0/0: local exit interface.
  • 00:10:15: age (10 minutes 15 seconds).

Troubleshooting: missing expected OSPF route? Check (1) show ip ospf neighbor — is the neighbor Full? (2) show ip ospf database — is the LSA in the LSDB? (3) show running-config on the source router — is the network advertised?

hack.memory: 'show ip route ospf' = OSPF-only routes. Codes: O (intra-area), O IA (inter-area), E1 (metric grows), E2 (metric fixed), O* (candidate default). Always check metric sum vs expected.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure OSPF, then run 'show ip route ospf' and identify every code for every route. Manually sum interface costs along a path and verify the metric matches. Add 'default-information originate' on the edge; observe O*E2 appear.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24-26. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20-21. Exam: code interpretation + metric calculation.

3.4.i.info:
show ip protocols displays all running IP routing protocols on the router, including OSPF, EIGRP, RIP, BGP. For OSPF specifically, it's a quick overview of process configuration, networks advertised, passive interfaces, and routing sources.

OSPF-relevant fields in the output:

  • Routing Protocol is 'ospf 1': process ID.
  • Outgoing update filter list for all interfaces is not set.
  • Incoming update filter list for all interfaces is not set.
  • Router ID X.X.X.X: active Router ID.
  • Number of areas: count of areas this router participates in.
  • Maximum path: N: ECMP paths (default 4; configurable with maximum-paths).
  • Routing for Networks: list of network commands (if used) showing IP+wildcard+area.
  • Routing on Interfaces Configured Explicitly: list of ip ospf [pid] area [id] commands (if used).
  • Passive Interface(s): list of all passive interfaces.
  • Routing Information Sources: list of neighbor RIDs and IPs, with last update time and AD.
  • Distance: (default is 110): default OSPF AD.

Example output:

R1# show ip protocols
*** IP Routing is NSF aware ***
Routing Protocol is 'ospf 1'
  Router ID 1.1.1.1
  Number of areas in this router is 1. 1 normal 0 stub 0 nssa
  Maximum path: 4
  Routing for Networks:
    10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 area 0
    192.168.1.0 0.0.0.255 area 0
  Passive Interface(s):
    GigabitEthernet0/2
  Routing Information Sources:
    Gateway     Distance  Last Update
    2.2.2.2     110       00:10:15
  Distance: (default is 110)

Quick diagnostic value: one command to see process ID, RID, networks, passive interfaces, neighbors, and AD — everything you need for 30-second OSPF sanity check.

hack.memory: 'show ip protocols' = one-stop shop for all routing protocol config. For OSPF: RID, networks, passive interfaces, neighbors, AD. Use as your OSPF sanity check after configuration. Always follow up with 'show ip ospf neighbor.'
hack.practice: After every OSPF lab: run 'show ip protocols' and verify every section — correct process ID, RID, networks, passive interfaces, and neighbor list. If anything is missing or wrong, investigate specifically before running other show commands.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam: output interpretation for OSPF process config.
